critic_raw: The RNN vs. logistic regression readmission prediction experiment on 200,000 visits has one concern: the primary metric is recall at 80% precision, with the threshold to achieve 80% precision set on the validation set. If the positive rate in the test set shifts relative to the validation set due to temporal distribution shift, the validation-derived threshold may not achieve 80% precision on test. The design should specify that achieved test precision is reported alongside test recall.
all_issues_raised: Recall at 80% precision requires threshold transfer from validation to test; if readmission rate shifts between these temporal windows, the threshold-derived precision on test may deviate from 80%, making the primary metric non-comparable between models.

round_transcripts.critic: The RNN vs. logistic regression readmission prediction experiment on 200,000 visits has one concern: the primary metric is recall at 80% precision, with the threshold to achieve 80% precision set on the validation set. If the positive rate in the test set shifts relative to the validation set due to temporal distribution shift, the validation-derived threshold may not achieve 80% precision on test. The design should specify that achieved test precision is reported alongside test recall.
round_transcripts.defender: The design explicitly lists AUROC and AUPRC as secondary metrics, providing threshold-free complements to the primary recall-at-precision metric. The recommended fix — reporting achieved test precision alongside test recall — is both straightforward and already implied by standard practice: any paper reporting recall at a fixed precision level would report the achieved precision to confirm the target was met. The large dataset size (~34,000 test records) also makes the threshold transfer more stable than in small-sample designs. The design should explicitly add 'report achieved test precision at the threshold selected on validation' as a reporting requirement, which addresses the concern fully.
